nativeRange.habitat: Midwater zones of large and medium-sized rivers and natural lakes

diet.notes: Readily accepts most aquarium foods — quality flakes, pellets, frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and Artemia. Larger specimens also benefit from plant matter including peas and spinach. Has been known to consume small invertebrates and very small fish.

avoidWith: small fish or shrimp that may be eaten
timid or slow-moving species intimidated by constant activity
fin-nippers that may target their flowing fins

summary: A large, active schooling cyprinid from SE Asia — not a true shark. Needs 600L+ and a group of 5+ adults; routinely dumped when outgrown. IUCN Endangered in the wild; trade specimens are almost entirely captive-bred.
careNotes:
CRITICAL: Bala Sharks are among the most commonly abandoned large fish in the hobby. Juveniles are sold at 3–5 cm but have been known to reach 30 cm in as little as two years, quickly outgrowing typical home aquaria. A tight-fitting, heavy lid is essential — this species is a powerful and skittish jumper that can launch itself from the tank at the slightest disturbance. Groups of fewer than four or five tend to develop internal bullying hierarchies; solitary specimens or pairs often become stressed or aggressive.

Wild populations are listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List (assessed 1996; populations have declined sharply in Borneo, Sumatra, Malay Peninsula, and the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins due to over-collection and habitat loss). Virtually 100% of trade specimens are farm-raised, mainly in Southeast Asia using hormone-induced spawning — captive breeding in home aquaria has not been documented reliably.

A long tank footprint (at least 200 cm / ~6.5 ft) is considered more important than total volume alone because this is a highly active, midwater, open-water swimmer that needs room to turn at speed. Water quality should be high, with good flow and regular maintenance. Despite their shark-like appearance they are generally peaceful toward fish too large to be swallowed, though their vigorous, active feeding can stress shy or slow-moving tankmates.
breedingNotes: Not reliably bred in home aquaria. Commercial farms use hormone-induced spawning in large ponds. Sexing adults is difficult; no reliable external dimorphism is documented. Hobbyists should not expect to breed this species.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Primary cross-verification source. Provided full tank dimensions (240×60 cm base), hardness range (36–268 ppm ≈ 2–15 dH), temperature floor of 20°C (averaged with other sources to 22°C), group minimum of 5, and detailed diet notes. Also confirmed IUCN Endangered status and hormone-farmed trade supply.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, dH 5–12, max size 35 cm SL, native range (Mekong, Chao Phraya, Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo), order Cypriniformes, family Cyprinidae.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Confirmed lifespan 8–10 years, skittish escape behaviour, shoaling minimum of 5, care level advanced.

fish.breedingNotes: Not reliably bred in home aquaria. Commercial farms use hormone-induced spawning in large outdoor ponds. No reliable sexing criteria for hobbyists.
